regulator: Central Bank of the UAE (CBUAE) -- this pillar is CBUAE-owned exclusively; VARA, the CMA, the DFSA, and the FSRA have no banking- or central-bank-money-specific regime of their own, and this card does not attribute any banking/money rule to them
instruments: CBUAE, Payment Token Services Regulation (Circular No. C 2/2024) -- confirmed by direct fetch of its live text on the CBUAE Rulebook, https://rulebook.centralbank.ae/en/rulebook/payment-token-services-regulation, which the Rulebook itself headers as 'C 2/2024 Effective from 31/8/2024 Status: In-Force'; under the Central Bank of the UAE's regulation, this Regulation applies UAE-wide but is expressly carved out of the Financial Free Zones for its core prohibitions -- Article (2)13 states verbatim, 'For the avoidance of doubt, reference to the UAE in this Article (2) excludes the jurisdiction of the Financial Free Zones,' and the Regulation's Applicant/License provisions require incorporation in the UAE under Article (74) of the Central Bank Law, with an entity 'incorporated and located outside of the UAE (which for the purposes of this Regulation would include a juridical person incorporated and located in a Financial Free Zone)' -- i.e. a DIFC or ADGM entity -- routed instead to the separate Foreign Payment Token Issuer Registration track, not a full License

CBUAE, Payment Token Services Regulation, Article (2)8 -- the direct banking-side restriction: 'A Bank may not act as a Payment Token Issuer, but may, subject to the licensing and other requirements of this regulation, set up a subsidiary, affiliate or other related entity to perform this activity,' confirmed verbatim by direct fetch of the same live Rulebook text

CBUAE, Payment Token Services Regulation, Article (8)2 and Article (8)11 -- the banking-side conversion route: 'A Bank or Exchange House may apply for a Non-Objection Registration in order to perform Dirham Payment Token Conversion,' with Article (8)11 separately listing which of the Regulation's other Articles (Articles 10, 11, 12, 17(2), 18, 23, and others through Article 38) a Registered Payment Token Conversion Provider that is a Bank or Exchange House must comply with; confirmed verbatim by direct fetch of the same live Rulebook text

CBUAE, Payment Token Services Regulation, Article (1) Definitions -- defines 'Bank' as 'any juridical person licensed in accordance with the provisions of the Central Bank Law, to carry on the activity of taking deposits of all types, including Shari'ah-compliant deposits,' and defines 'Financial Free Zones' as 'free zones subject to the provisions of Federal Law No. (8) of 2004, regarding Financial Free Zones, as amended' -- confirmed verbatim by direct fetch

CBUAE, Payment Token Services Regulation, Article (40) -- defines a 'Transition Period' (Definitions, item 78: 'has the meaning given in Article (40)'), confirmed by direct fetch of the definitions list, but this session's fetch of the Regulation's live Rulebook text was truncated before reaching Article (40)'s substantive provisions, so the exact transition start/end dates could not be independently confirmed against the primary source this session -- secondary legal-commentary sources (not independently verified here) report a one-year transition period running from the Regulation's gazette publication to approximately June 2025, after which its prohibitions (including on Virtual Assets as a Means of Payment) took full effect; see open_items

CBUAE, 'Guidance for Licensed Financial Institutions on Risks Related to Virtual Assets and Virtual Asset Service Providers,' 20 February 2023, issued by the CBUAE's AML/CFT Supervision Department -- confirmed by direct fetch of the PDF, https://www.centralbank.ae/media/avwlktgy/cbuae-guidance-for-lfis-on-risks-related-to-virtual-assets-and-virtual-assets-providers_final-clean-version1.pdf; this is an AML/CFT risk-guidance document, not a prudential capital-adequacy instrument -- its stated purpose is to help CBUAE-licensed financial institutions ('LFIs': national banks, branches of foreign banks, exchange houses, finance companies, payment service providers, and registered hawala providers, plus insurance companies) meet their statutory AML/CFT obligations regarding virtual assets and VASP exposure; it states the Guidance 'neither constitutes additional legislation or regulation nor replaces or supersedes any legal or regulatory requirements,' and that under the Central Bank of the UAE's regulation, the CBUAE 'supervises LFIs and RHPs, including in their capacity as financial service providers to VASPs and to non-VASP customers that may engage in virtual asset (VA) transactions' -- this pillar found no separate CBUAE prudential/capital-adequacy notice specific to banks' virtual-asset exposure this session; see open_items

CBUAE press release, 'CBUAE launches the Central Bank Digital Currency Strategy "The Digital Dirham"', Abu Dhabi, 23 March 2023 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the PDF, https://www.centralbank.ae/media/q5nldmrv/cbuae-launches-the-central-bank-digital-currency-strategy-the-digital-dirham-en.pdf; announces the CBDC Strategy as one of nine initiatives of the CBUAE's Financial Infrastructure Transformation (FIT) Programme, with G42 Cloud and R3 engaged as infrastructure and technology providers, and states the first phase, 'expected to complete over the next 12 to 15 months,' comprises three pillars: a soft launch of mBridge for cross-border CBDC settlement, proof-of-concept work for a bilateral CBDC bridge with India, and proof-of-concept work for domestic CBDC issuance covering wholesale and retail usage

CBUAE, 'Digital Dirham -- A Primer on the UAE's Central Bank Digital Currency,' Policy Paper No. 1/2025, July 2025 -- confirmed by direct fetch and text extraction of the PDF, https://www.centralbank.ae/media/lczb23l4/cbdc-short-report_july.pdf; states the CBUAE 'will soon be launching the UAE's central bank digital currency (CBDC) -- the "Digital Dirham" in all formats (wholesale and retail)' and confirms Phase I of the CBDC project as completed, spanning 15 months (March 2023 - June 2024); records that in January 2024, HH Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan, Chairman of the CBUAE, authorised the first issuance of the Digital Dirham as legal tender for the first cross-border CBDC payment on the mBridge minimum viable product (MVP) platform (a collaboration among the BIS Innovation Hub, the Bank of Thailand, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority, the Digital Currency Institute of the People's Bank of China, and the CBUAE), and that mBridge has since reached MVP stage and is described by the CBUAE as 'the world's first operational multi-CBDC platform'; separately records a January 2024 real-value retail pilot conducted with several LFIs testing four use cases (fractional ownership of tokenised assets, a smart tourist wallet, smart social benefit payments via the Ministry of Community Development, and parent/child sub-wallets)

CBUAE, Policy Paper No. 1/2025 (July 2025), design features -- describes the Digital Dirham as distributed through an 'intermediated' two-tier model with hybrid wallet-based access combining account- and token-based approaches on a permissioned distributed ledger; wallet providers will include LFIs (banks, exchange houses, payment service providers) and FinTechs holding SVF (Stored Value Facility) licences; the Digital Dirham will launch as a non-interest-bearing, tiered CBDC with user holding/transaction limits, and will be fully fungible and convertible with other forms of the Dirham (cash, bank deposits); confirmed by direct fetch and text extraction of the same PDF; the paper states only that the CBUAE 'will advance preparations for the full launch of both the retail, wholesale and cross-border versions... in the coming years' and that 'the launch will be carried out in stages' -- it does not commit to a specific launch date, and this pillar does not adopt secondary reporting of specific 2025/2026 launch dates not found in this primary source; see open_items

CBUAE press release, 'Mansour bin Zayed executes first direct digital payment; witnesses launch of strategic initiatives to enhance financial cooperation, interlink financial infrastructures between UAE, China,' Abu Dhabi, 19 November 2025 -- confirmed by direct fetch and text extraction of the PDF, https://www.centralbank.ae/media/sbmdgxrw/mansour-bin-zayed-executes-first-direct-digital-payment-and-witnesses-the-launch-of-strategic-initiatives-en.pdf; records that HH Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan 'executed the first-ever cross-border payment using central bank digital currency (CBDC) between the UAE and China through the "Jisr" network, officially launching the platform,' that the Jisr platform was 'established with the participation of a group of Emirati and Chinese banks' and 'is expected to undergo further expansion in 2026, with the addition of more central banks,' and that CBUAE Governor Khaled Mohamed Balama and PBOC Governor Pan Gongsheng signed an MoU 'aimed at strengthening cooperation in cross-border payments, developing financial infrastructures, and supporting joint strategic initiatives'
standing_summary: Banking and central-bank money is exclusively the Central Bank of the UAE's (CBUAE's) pillar: none of the four market regulators covered elsewhere in this jurisdiction's cards -- VARA (Dubai mainland and its free zones, excluding the DIFC), the CMA (federal, outside the two financial free zones), the DFSA (DIFC only), or the FSRA (ADGM only) -- has its own banking- or central-bank-money-specific regime, and this card attributes no such rule to them. Two separate strands of CBUAE activity are covered here: the banking-side effects of the Payment Token Services Regulation, and the Digital Dirham CBDC programme. Payment Token Services Regulation, banking-side effects: under the Central Bank of the UAE's regulation (Circular No. C 2/2024, confirmed by direct fetch of the live CBUAE Rulebook text as 'Effective from 31/8/2024, Status: In-Force'), the Regulation applies UAE-wide but is expressly and verbatim carved out of the Financial Free Zones (DIFC and ADGM) for its core prohibitions (Article (2)13), and requires an Applicant for a full Payment Token Services License to be incorporated in the UAE outside those zones; a juridical person incorporated and located in a Financial Free Zone is treated, for the purposes of this Regulation, as 'outside of the UAE' and must instead pursue Foreign Payment Token Issuer Registration -- so DIFC- and ADGM-incorporated banks fall outside the CBUAE's direct Payment Token Services licensing perimeter under this instrument, consistent with those two zones' separate DFSA and FSRA regulatory regimes (covered in their own pillars, not duplicated here). Within its own scope, the Regulation directly restricts what UAE banks (onshore, non-free-zone) may do: Article (2)8 states verbatim that 'A Bank may not act as a Payment Token Issuer, but may, subject to the licensing and other requirements of this regulation, set up a subsidiary, affiliate or other related entity to perform this activity' -- so a UAE bank cannot itself issue a Dirham Payment Token or Foreign Payment Token, only do so indirectly through a separately licensed subsidiary or affiliate. Separately, Article (8)2 permits 'A Bank or Exchange House' to apply for a Non-Objection Registration to perform Dirham Payment Token Conversion, with Article (8)11 specifying which of the Regulation's other Articles (spanning Articles 10 through 38) apply to a Bank or Exchange House holding that registration. The Regulation defines 'Bank' precisely as 'any juridical person licensed in accordance with the provisions of the Central Bank Law, to carry on the activity of taking deposits of all types, including Shari'ah-compliant deposits.' The Regulation's own definitions confirm a 'Transition Period' exists (Article (40)), but this session's direct fetch of the Regulation's live text was truncated before reaching Article (40)'s substantive text, so this card does not assert the transition's exact start or end dates as primary-verified; see open_items for what secondary sources report. Banks' virtual-asset exposure -- AML/CFT guidance located, prudential/capital guidance not found: the only CBUAE guidance located and directly fetched this session addressing licensed financial institutions' (including banks') exposure to virtual assets is the CBUAE's 'Guidance for Licensed Financial Institutions on Risks Related to Virtual Assets and Virtual Asset Service Providers' (20 February 2023, issued by the CBUAE's AML/CFT Supervision Department). This is explicitly an AML/CFT risk-guidance document -- its own text states it 'neither constitutes additional legislation or regulation nor replaces or supersedes any legal or regulatory requirements,' and it sets CBUAE's expectations for how LFIs (defined to include national banks, branches of foreign banks, exchange houses, finance companies, payment service providers, and registered hawala providers, alongside insurance companies) should manage money-laundering and terrorist-financing risk arising from their own or their customers' virtual-asset activity and from banking relationships with VASPs; it is not a capital-adequacy or prudential-exposure-limit instrument. This card found no separate CBUAE prudential (capital-treatment) notice specific to banks' virtual-asset holdings this session and does not assert one exists; the AML/CFT-focused guidance is described accurately as such, not conflated with prudential regulation. Digital Dirham (CBDC): under the Central Bank of the UAE's regulation, the CBUAE launched its CBDC Strategy, branded 'The Digital Dirham,' on 23 March 2023 as one of nine initiatives under its Financial Infrastructure Transformation (FIT) Programme, engaging G42 Cloud and R3 as infrastructure and technology providers, with a stated first phase (12-15 months) comprising three pillars: an mBridge soft launch for cross-border CBDC settlement, proof-of-concept work for a bilateral CBDC bridge with India, and proof-of-concept domestic CBDC issuance covering both wholesale and retail usage. The CBUAE's own July 2025 Policy Paper No. 1/2025 confirms Phase I as completed (a 15-month span, March 2023 to June 2024): in January 2024 the CBUAE authorised the first issuance of the Digital Dirham as legal tender and completed the first cross-border CBDC payment on the mBridge minimum viable product (MVP) platform -- a collaboration with the BIS Innovation Hub, the Bank of Thailand, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority, and the Digital Currency Institute of the People's Bank of China -- which the CBUAE now describes as having reached MVP stage and become 'the world's first operational multi-CBDC platform'; the same month, a real-value retail pilot was run with several licensed financial institutions testing four use cases (fractional ownership of tokenised real-estate assets, a smart tourist wallet with programmable spending and instant VAT refunds, government smart-social-benefit payments distributed by the Ministry of Community Development, and parent/child sub-wallets). The Digital Dirham's planned design, per the same primary source, uses an intermediated two-tier distribution model with hybrid account- and token-based wallet access on a permissioned distributed ledger; LFIs (banks, exchange houses, payment service providers) and SVF-licensed FinTechs will act as wallet providers; the currency will launch non-interest-bearing, tiered by holding/transaction limit, and fully fungible and convertible with cash and ordinary bank deposits. The July 2025 paper states the CBUAE 'will advance preparations for the full launch of both the retail, wholesale and cross-border versions... in the coming years' and that the rollout 'will be carried out in stages,' without committing to a specific date in that primary source; this card does not adopt secondary media reports of a specific 2025 or 2026 launch date that could not be corroborated against a CBUAE primary source this session -- see open_items. Most recently, a CBUAE press release dated 19 November 2025 records that HH Sheikh Mansour bin Zayed Al Nahyan, CBUAE Chairman, executed 'the first-ever cross-border payment using central bank digital currency (CBDC) between the UAE and China' through the newly launched 'Jisr' platform, established with the participation of a group of Emirati and Chinese banks and expected to expand further in 2026 with additional participating central banks; the same event saw a Memorandum of Understanding signed between the CBUAE and the People's Bank of China governors on cross-border payment cooperation. This card does not independently verify whether the Digital Dirham CBDC programme's scope extends into, or is carved out of, the DIFC and ADGM financial free zones -- none of the primary CBUAE sources fetched this session addresses that question directly, and this card does not assume an answer; see open_items.

key_links.label: CBUAE Rulebook: Payment Token Services Regulation (C 2/2024, Effective from 31/8/2024, In-Force) -- full text including Article (2)8 (Bank issuer restriction), Article (8)2/(8)11 (Bank/Exchange House conversion Non-Objection Registration), and Article (2)13 (Financial Free Zone carve-out)
CBUAE: Guidance for Licensed Financial Institutions on Risks Related to Virtual Assets and Virtual Asset Service Providers (20 February 2023, AML/CFT Supervision Department) -- PDF
CBUAE press release: CBUAE launches the Central Bank Digital Currency Strategy 'The Digital Dirham' (23 March 2023) -- PDF
CBUAE: Digital Dirham -- A Primer on the UAE's Central Bank Digital Currency, Policy Paper No. 1/2025 (July 2025) -- PDF, design features and Phase I completion status
CBUAE: Digital Dirham -- Selected Issues and Policy Considerations, companion long-form policy paper (July 2025) -- PDF
CBUAE press release: Mansour bin Zayed executes first direct digital payment; launch of the 'Jisr' CBDC platform between UAE and China (19 November 2025) -- PDF
U.ae (Official Platform of the UAE Government): Central Bank Digital Currency Strategy -- the Digital Dirham, official overview page

open_items: This card could not independently confirm the exact start and end dates of the Payment Token Services Regulation's Article (40) 'Transition Period' against primary source text this session: the definitions list (item 78) confirms the term is defined in Article (40), but the direct fetch of the Regulation's live Rulebook text was truncated before reaching Article (40) itself, and repeated attempts to reach the specific article page returned HTTP 403 or an unrelated section. Secondary legal-commentary sources (not independently verified here) report a one-year transition period running from gazettal to approximately 14 June 2025, after which the Regulation's prohibitions (including on using Virtual Assets as a Means of Payment) took full effect. This should be re-verified against the primary Article (40) text before being treated as confirmed.

This card found only an AML/CFT-focused CBUAE guidance document (20 February 2023) addressing LFIs' (including banks') virtual-asset exposure, and found no separate CBUAE prudential or capital-adequacy notice specific to banks' virtual-asset holdings this session. Given the CBUAE's active supervisory role (Basel-based capital framework generally applies to UAE banks), a dedicated prudential/capital-treatment instrument for banks' crypto or payment-token exposure may exist but was not located and fetched this session; this should be a target for a future research pass rather than assumed absent.

The Digital Dirham CBDC programme's applicability to, or carve-out from, the DIFC and ADGM financial free zones was not addressed in any of the primary CBUAE sources (the March 2023 launch release, the July 2025 policy papers, or the November 2025 Jisr press release) fetched this session. Given this pillar's binding instruction to verify free-zone carve-outs live rather than assume them, this card makes no claim about whether Digital Dirham wallets, issuance, or the Jisr network extend into or exclude the DIFC/ADGM -- an open question for a future pass, ideally cross-checked against DFSA and FSRA sources in their own pillars.

Secondary media reporting (not independently verified against a CBUAE primary source this session) states more specific Digital Dirham rollout dates than the CBUAE's own July 2025 policy paper commits to -- variously a 'October to December 2025' target and a 'March 2026' retail go-live date, plus a claim that CBUAE has 'decreed Digital Dirham adoption by all UAE licensed financial institutions by 2026.' None of these specific dates or the adoption-mandate claim could be corroborated against a primary CBUAE document fetched this session; this card reports only what the CBUAE's own 23 March 2023 launch release, July 2025 policy paper, and 19 November 2025 Jisr press release state directly, and treats the more specific secondary-reported dates as unverified.

A separate November 2025 announcement, reported via the UAE Ministry of Finance's own website (mof.gov.ae), describes 'the first government financial transaction using Digital Dirham' between the Ministry of Finance and Dubai Finance, distinct from the CBUAE-issued 19 November 2025 Jisr press release covered in this card's instruments. This card does not include that MOF-sourced claim as an instrument because MOF is not this pillar's operative regulator (CBUAE is) and this card's fetch of mof.gov.ae returned only a processed summary, not verbatim primary text suitable for direct citation under this project's sourcing standard; it is flagged here rather than asserted, pending either a CBUAE-hosted primary source for the same event or a decision that it duplicates the Jisr platform launch already covered.

The general proposition that the Central Bank Law (Federal Decree-Law No. (14) of 2018, as amended) itself applies UAE-wide outside the Financial Free Zones -- consistent with, and the likely statutory basis for, the Payment Token Services Regulation's own Article (2)13 carve-out -- is corroborated only by law-firm secondary commentary (e.g. CMS Law's summary of the 2023 Central Bank Law amendments) this session, not by an independent direct fetch of the Central Bank Law's own text. The Payment Token Services Regulation's own carve-out (Article (2)13) is primary-verified directly; the broader Central Bank Law scope claim is not, and should be re-verified against the Law's own text before being treated as independently confirmed.

Stablecoin-specific issuer eligibility, reserve-asset requirements, and the Dirham Payment Token vs. Foreign Payment Token licensing/registration distinction under the same Payment Token Services Regulation are tracked in full in the Stablecoins pillar; this pillar covers only the Regulation's banking-side provisions (Articles (2)8 and (8)2/(8)11) and does not duplicate that fuller treatment.
